text: Okay, firstly, let me thank the organizers, especially Dr. Prashant for inviting me. I will just show one case, just a thirty eight year old lady thirty-eight year old lady first saw me in August, two thousand and eight. She complained of severe pelvic pain and she had an endometrioma and adenomyotic uterus. And I did a laparoscopic cystectomy for her and subsequently she underwent a laparoscopic cystectomy and then she was lost to follow up. She was seen in August, two thousand and nineteen complaining of severe dysmenorrhea, heavy menses and pelvic pain. This time she had a right endometrioma measuring thirteen point four nine centimeters and eight point nine six centimeters and a uterus was large, a sixteen week size uterus. So she underwent a total laparoscopic hysterectomy.

The surgeon places a trocar directly into the cyst and aspirates its contents. Blunt dissection is attempted to release the ovary, but significant adhesions impede progress, preventing access to the ureters.

The surgeon encounters significant adhesions between the gut, ovaries, and uterus due to previous surgeries. The round ligament is grasped, and the peritoneum is opened anteriorly, superiorly, and inferiorly in an attempt to access the uterine artery at its origin. The surgeon identifies a tubular structure resembling a ureter but is uncertain. Attempts to locate the uterine artery near the obliterated umbilical artery are unsuccessful, prompting a change in approach to release the ovaries and intestines from the uterus.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection to release the left ovary from the pelvic side wall, holding onto the infundibulopelvic ligament. Despite difficulty in visualizing the anatomy, the left ureter is eventually identified. The surgeon continues to dissect the left ovary from pelvic adhesions, aiming to separate the uterus from the gut.

The surgeon opens the round ligament on the right side and releases the anterior peritoneum, encountering minimal endometriosis or adhesions. The gut is carefully separated from the uterus to create more space for surgical manipulation, although the large and adherent uterus remains difficult to move despite the presence of a uterine manipulator.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection to release the gut from the uterus, followed by sharp dissection with scissors to separate the rectum from the uterus due to severe adhesions. Anteriorly, the bladder is successfully released. The surgeon attempts to access the uterine arteries on the right side but encounters difficulty. The left infundibulopelvic ligament is targeted next, followed by dissection of the left broad ligament, with awareness of the ureter's location aiding the process. Despite efforts, the uterus remains immobile, prompting further dissection at the posterior aspect.

The surgeon releases the right ovarian cyst containing endometriosis, followed by sharp dissection to separate the bowel from the uterus amidst significant adhesions. The dissection is bloody but controlled, revealing the left and right pararectal spaces. Due to the malfunctioning uterine manipulator, vasopressin is injected into the uterus, and a myoma spiral is used as an alternative manipulator.

The surgeon uses a myoma spiral and a suprapubic port to assist in the dissection. The left ovary is carefully dissected away from the sigmoid colon using blunt dissection, revealing the pararectal space. The left ovary is released from adhesions, but the surgeon decides to leave it behind temporarily to focus on removing the uterus. The left pararectal space is dissected, allowing clear visualization of the uterine artery and proper dissection of the left ureter.

The surgeon proceeds to coagulate and cut the right ascending branch of the uterine artery, ensuring the ureter is safely dissected out. The medial aspect of the ovary is visible during this process. Due to limited space, the surgeon opts for coagulation instead of suturing the uterine artery.

The surgeon creates a window in the right broad ligament and dissects the ureter, which is adherent to the ovary. Despite difficulty, the ureter is successfully isolated, but the ovary remains attached. The infundibulopelvic ligament is coagulated and cut, and the ovarian ligament is similarly treated to separate the ovary from the uterus, leaving the ovaries behind.

The surgeon releases the ovary from the uterus and cuts the uterosacral ligament. The ureter is visible and dissected out, allowing the uterine arteries to be coagulated and cut. The vagina is opened using coagulation, with the bladder pushed down to facilitate the procedure.

The surgeon opens the vagina over a vaginal tube and holds the cervix, making a circumferential incision to detach the uterus. Using a harmonic scalpel, the uterus is completely detached and removed. The surgeon then proceeds to remove the remaining ovaries and ovarian tissues, addressing adhesions and diseases on the bowel. A serosal tear is noted for later repair. The right ovary, containing a large cyst embedded in the pouch of Douglas, is dissected out using blunt and sharp dissection.

The surgeon releases the ovary from dense adhesions in the pelvis, successfully excising it. The large right endometrioma is removed, and the left ovary is released from its adherence to the rectum. The ureter is clearly visible, allowing for the removal of remaining endometriotic tissue. The surgeon enters the left pararectal space to separate the rectum from the pelvic side wall, facilitating the excision of the ovary.

The surgeon uses sharp scissors near the bowel and a harmonic scalpel away from vital structures to gently release the ovary from the pelvis, ensuring no ovarian or endometriotic tissue is left behind. The ovary is removed, and adherent tissues on the vaginal vault and rectum are meticulously dissected. Endometriosis is shaved off the bowel, and a serosal defect is repaired with 3-0 polyglycine sutures, concluding the surgery.

The surgeon closes the vaginal wall using figure-of-eight sutures at the angles, followed by mattress sutures to secure the vaginal vault. The rectum is tested for leakage, and the right and left ureters are visible, marking the conclusion of the surgery.

Okay, so this is the vaginal vault and the uterus actually measured one kilogram, although it's not a big uterus. So this is my last slide. So thank you to IAGE for inviting me. There are all my teachers here, Roshan, Deepak is also my teacher, Kurian is also here, Anut Vathaya. So thank you very much and back to you.
